digest.intro: Two maritime corridors close in one week; AI agents find things nobody meant to show.

items.title: The Strait of Hormuz Becomes a Toll Road
items.blurb: Traffic through the Strait of Hormuz — normally ~130 ships per day carrying a fifth of global oil — collapsed to 14 ships on Sunday after Trump ordered the US military to destroy Iranian air defenses, radar, and small-boat capacity. He then announced a 20% fee on cargo from any country that wants US protection through the strait: a straight-faced attempt to convert international waters into a protection racket. Iran says it manages the strait; the UAE says Iran just attacked two tankers anyway, killing one person.

items.title: Ukraine's Drones Closed the Sea of Azov in Under a Week
items.blurb: Ukraine's Unmanned Systems Forces struck more than 100 Russian tankers and ships per night from July 6–13, forcing a complete shutdown of the Don River–Azov corridor and halting all Kerch Strait transits — effectively re-isolating Crimea from seaborne fuel resupply. About a quarter of Russia's grain exports move through that corridor, and wheat prices have started rising. It's the clearest proof yet that cheap one-way drones can do what a conventional navy would take years and billions to accomplish.

items.title: Germany Is Quietly Gutting Its Freedom of Information Law
items.blurb: Buried at item 34 of a 34-point reform package pushed through just before parliament's summer recess, Germany's ruling CDU/CSU-SPD coalition is moving to effectively abolish the IFG — the 2006 law that lets journalists, NGOs, and citizens demand government data freely and quickly. The stated justification is cybersecurity and the sensitivity of government data in an era of global cyberwarfare, but critics say that rationale could swallow the entire transparency regime. Germany is a democratic bellwether; if this passes without serious pushback, expect the template to travel.

items.title: An AI Agent Accidentally Leaked the Fields Medal Winners
items.blurb: Someone ran a Codex-generated curl command against the ICM 2026 schedule and found four lecture slots marked "HIDDEN" in the front-end code — inside: Yu Deng, John Pardon, Jacob Tsimerman, and Hong Wang, apparently the Fields Medal class of 2026. If confirmed on July 23, Hong Wang would be only the third woman in the medal's history to receive it, and this would be the first time two Chinese mathematicians won in the same cycle. The parable: AI agents are very good at finding things you forgot to actually hide.

items.title: OpenAI's Codex Encrypted Its Own Sub-Agent Prompts — Now Nobody Can Audit Them
items.blurb: A merged PR (#26210) made Codex's MultiAgentV2 inter-agent messages opaque: the content field is now left empty and only an encrypted payload is stored, meaning even the parent agent can't read what instructions were sent to its children. The practical problem: you can no longer answer basic questions like "what task did this spawn_agent call give the child?" or "why did this subagent thread exist?" The deeper problem: AI opacity is no longer a philosophical concern — it's now a design choice being shipped in version numbers.

items.title: DOOMQL: SQLite as the Actual Game Engine
items.blurb: Peter Gostev built a Doom-like game where SQLite is not the save file but the full engine: SQL queries handle movement, collision detection, enemy logic, combat, and every RGB pixel on screen via a ray tracer implemented as a recursive CTE. You can run Datasette against the live game database while playing and watch the frame_pixels view update in real time, then ask Claude to add a minimap. It started as a deliberately unreasonable question and ended up being weirdly complete.

items.title: The Most Detailed 3D Atlas of the Human Brainstem
items.blurb: Scientists at IIT Madras stitched together 500+ tissue sections from foetal, childhood, and adult brains into ANCHOR — a cellular-resolution 3D atlas of the brainstem that lets you zoom from a whole-brain MRI down to individual nerve cells without leaving the model. It maps 200+ cell clusters and pathways using eight chemical markers, all from high-resolution microscopy rather than expensive molecular techniques. The brainstem is a sliver of tissue that keeps you breathing and conscious; until now, pathologists diagnosing disorders there were still working from scattered 2D slices.

items.title: The Arm Spin That Stops a Fall
items.blurb: Steve Mould takes the reflex everyone has felt — windmilling your arms when you start to slip — and traces it back to conservation of angular momentum as an embodied human control system. Mould is reliably good at making abstract physics feel like something you can feel in your body. Short, and you will immediately want to spin your arms.

items.title: Sketching India Before the Camera Arrived
items.blurb: In the late 1830s, Emily Eden traveled across northern India with her brother the governor-general and drew everyone: princes, fakirs, servants, Akali warriors, hill communities, Afghan nobles — a visual record that would otherwise simply not exist, since photography hadn't arrived yet. Her 1844 publication, Portraits of the Princes and People of India, is now the centerpiece of an exhibition in Delhi, reassembled as a complete series of hand-colored lithographs. What makes it strange is the breadth of her gaze: imperial-adjacent but not imperial in subject, a document of the overlooked made by someone with full access to the celebrated.

facts.fact: Before this week's conflict, roughly 130 ships per day transited the Strait of Hormuz; on Sunday that number was 14.

Ukraine's drone campaign struck over 100 Russian ships per night for seven consecutive nights and shut down an entire maritime corridor — something no traditional naval blockade had achieved in the conflict.
If Hong Wang is confirmed as a 2026 Fields Medal recipient, she will be only the third woman in the award's history — which began in 1936 — to receive it.
Germany's IFG transparency law, in effect since 2006, is being targeted for abolition via item 34 of a 34-point reform package slipped through just before parliamentary summer recess.

ANCHOR, the new 3D brainstem atlas from IIT Madras, was built from over 500 tissue sections spanning foetal through adult brains and identifies more than 200 distinct clusters of cells — all using standard microscopy rather than expensive molecular techniques.

art_picks.title: Sans Soleil
art_picks.creator: Chris Marker
art_picks.why: A film that functions as a philosophy of memory and image-making — Marker wanders Tokyo, Guinea-Bissau, and Iceland asking how cultures encode time and ritual into everyday surfaces. It's the essay-film as operating system, and it will rewire how you look at cities.

art_picks.title: Untitled (Your body is a battleground)
art_picks.creator: Barbara Kruger
art_picks.why: Kruger fuses the grammar of advertising with the logic of power — text as weapon, image as territory. If you care about systems that quietly shape identity and desire, her work is the place where graphic design becomes political philosophy at full volume.

art_picks.title: Souvlaki
art_picks.creator: Slowdive
art_picks.why: The record that proves texture is structure — every wash of reverb and delay is load-bearing. For someone who thinks about physical and digital space, it's a masterclass in building immersive environments out of nothing but layered signal.

events.name: Nicola Vassell Gallery: "Follow Through"
events.venue: Nicola Vassell Gallery, 138 Tenth Ave, Chelsea
events.why: A tight group show with Alvaro Barrington, Na Kim, Ming Smith, and Uman among others — artists whose practices blur identity, community, and image-making; strong signal-to-noise ratio for a Chelsea summer.

events.name: Gabriel Orozco: Public Nature
events.venue: JCDecaux Bus Shelters across New York City
events.why: Orozco's first commissioned photographic series — 10 new site-specific images about urban fabric distributed across bus shelters, turning the city's own infrastructure into gallery walls; the work finds you rather than the reverse.

events.name: Pierre Huyghe: UUmwelt
events.venue: MoMA Abby Aldrich Rockefeller Sculpture Garden, 11 W 53rd St, Manhattan
events.why: An AI trained on fMRI brain-scan data generates hallucinatory imagery in real time, activated by your own gaze — the piece literally changes based on who's looking at it, dissolving the boundary between human and machine cognition in a public garden.

events.name: David Byrne / Saul Steinberg: Influence and Affinity
events.why: A pairing of two obsessives who turned everyday observation into full-blown cosmologies — Steinberg's graphic systems meet Byrne's cultural promiscuity; exactly the kind of category-blurring dialogue that rewards close looking.

events.name: MoMA PS1 Warm Up 2026 — Opening Night
events.venue: MoMA PS1 Courtyard, 22-25 Jackson Ave, Long Island City, Queens
events.why: The 28th season opens with Carlos Souffront, a Wackies Showcase pairing Bronx reggae label founder Lloyd Barnes with Basic Channel's Mark Ernestus — dub-techno genealogy performed live in a museum courtyard at dusk; the lineup this year is unusually deep on subculture lineage.
